speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] is Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. This is roll 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I came, and I went-- I ran home. Next day was, again, was a Friday. And that was the famous day where we had to spend at that place again. Each one had to show them the passes, that means the [INAUDIBLE] with this big, green stamp on it. My sister had hers. I had mine. And the two of us walked. And thousands of people were present there. And this is when we realized, that the day we realized, that it was not just for old people. We realized they mean children too. Because actual this was an aktion for children. Anyone who had a child with them was taken away, be the man or a woman. People did not know what to expect, really and actual, we did not know what's going on, what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So naturally people took their children with them. What they going to do with it? And an awful lot went. The man who was running that factory for which I worked at that time, his wife had given birth. The baby was about two or three weeks old, was the apple of her eye, naturally, like every mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Nazi, one of the officers, picked up that-- years ago, we used to keep the babies in, like, little pillows, you know, wrapped up and tied up-- he just picked up that baby's pillow, touched the baby's neck, and gave the baby back to the mother, and took her on the side. The baby was dead. He killed the child and made sure that she is holding the child to take her away. And then who used to work, he was the valet for Mr. Grunow. Grunow was the-- he was the head of Tarnow, not only for the Jews alone. He was the head of the Gestapo. And he was the main person, Grunow, and this Jew was his valet, because he knew how to drive. He was a tailor by trade, so he knew how to fix his clothes. He knew how to iron his clothes. He knew how to drive him around. And he was the apple of his eye. But that Jew, that big, tremendous Jew, he must have been at least 220 pounds. He must have been six feet five. I still see him before my eyes. That Jew had a two-year-old child with him, and he refused to give that child away. He refused to just hand over that little girl to his wife. And that Nazi, not Grunow, but the other Nazi, insisted. And he told his dog Uda, I not own one dog ran at him, but two. They were trying to go for the jugular. And that man toppled, and the dogs had a feast. And that little girl ran over to her father, crying, in Polish, Daddy, Daddy, my Daddy, kissing his face. And I guess even this was too much for Grunow, because he had a heart at that moment and shot that little child on top of-- laying and kissing dead father. It's a picture that I guess I'll never forget. I still see it before my eyes. Those dogs drinking the blood, and that little child laying there on her father, on her father's face. I still see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those are little incidents that did not just-- it was a little different, each day made a different, each incident made it a little different. Do you know if Grunow was ever brought to justice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I don't know. Maybe he was killed by-- maybe he killed himself. He was a drunkard. I don't think that any of them were doing things under normal condition. I think that these Nazis, either they were drunk or they were on dope.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't think they were following higher orders when they did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were following higher orders, yes. But to follow that order and be so inhuman, you had to be either a drunk or out of your skull. Because normal people don't do things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back for a moment. The day before, when another Nazi said to you, run. Do you have any idea why he said to you to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. Maybe he followed the order of that other one. Maybe the other one that gave me that stamp was more important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was taken away that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters were-- two sisters were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sisters were taken that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pola and Rachel. My oldest sister had her stamp. She was at work that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pola and Rachel, did you ever see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But a few days later, I was called to the Jewish police headquarters. There was a card for me that came by mail. The stamp was from Przeworsk, which is the big town, big town, that was the town near Belzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to identify the handwriting, because it was my sister's, my sister Rachel. She had written that card. Who knows when she had written? She only wrote that we are in a place here, we are hungry and thirsty, but we are all right. And she mentioned her name, and Pola's name, and two friends, and a cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that must have been written under duress, or whatever it was done, however it was done. Most likely it was done under duress. And then they were killed in Belzec. Was Because Belzec, nobody escaped. I also read somewheres in the together pamphlets that they send me every so often from New York, that somebody-- that two people escaped. They wrote a book about it, but I have no idea if it's true or not. But Belzec-- you see, Treblinka, some people escaped. Majdanek, some people survived, because these people were brought back to Plaszow. But whether anybody-- I don't think anyone survived Belzec. That was that. And then Madritch came to town, to Tarnow. I had a sewing machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madritch was a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a factory of clothing and shirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-D-R-I-T-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a German factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a German name and a German factory. And we-- and he had-- he was a contractor for the army's clothes, for the army's linen, sheets, pillowcases, shirts, underwear, and eventual was uniforms also, some uniforms, not the fancy ones, but some. And Madritch, comparing to Mr. Schindler-- I never met Mr. Schindler. But he was trying to do something good for his workers. We used to get an extra piece of bread on a weekly basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Madritch?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Madritch, used to send an extra few breads, so each one received an extra piece of bread. In Plaszow, all right, we'll come to that. But even in town of ghetto, he made sure that we did not suffer too much of the hunger. And we were taken every day by Jewish police and by the Nazis, with dogs, naturally, through the streets, from the ghetto, to go and work in the factory. It was 12 hour shift. Daytime or nighttime, it lasted 12 hours. You had a half a day on a Sunday off. All right, at least we were breathing, we were-- quote unquote, we were alive. After the incident, there was a Sunday, November 15 of '42, early morning. My sister's shift was early morning. I had a day off from work. I have to have a day off from work. And all of a sudden, there was an aktion, spontaneous thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bunker in that building where we were living. But I don't know what transpired. I said to myself, I'm working for Madritch. I have the stamp. What more will they want from me? I did not bother hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, somehow, I had such a premonition of something that I pick myself up, I put my coat on. It was cold. It was wintertime. And there was a little-- like years ago, that was used as a sukkah, as a built sukkah on that floor, was on the second floor of the building. And it was abandoned. It wasn't used. It was-- people were throwing things that they couldn't use into that. And I don't know. I heard the Nazis hollering already. I walked in there, and I crouched down, and I sat down there. Some reason, I covered myself. I was out of sight. Somebody walks in, a mother with a child. She lived on the third floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says to me, I'll walk out if you don't want me here. And I said to her, you know what? Why should you? If I'll go, I'll go. And that child started crying. She had given her a tranquilizer prior to coming down, but she didn't stop crying. And the Germans are hollering.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they opened up the bunker. Somebody snitched about that bunker. And they took out 30 some-odd people, it was about 12 children, out of there. And the screaming and the hollering downstairs were horrendous. Beatings-- it was just unreal. And I said to my-- they're going to come. And I see the shoes, walking, and looking, and looking. The baby, for the few minutes, stopped crying. And they're walking, looking for that baby that was crying, that they heard the baby cry. They didn't find us. God was with us. They didn't find us. And we stayed there for quite a while before we dared leave that spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been toward evening that the Jewish policeman who was a cousin, who was related to these people that we shared the apartment with, he was calling my name. He was calling my name. Where are you? Where are you? Where are you? And I dared stand up. He said, it's over. The aktion is over. They are away. I'm glad you're here. Your sister sent a message. She didn't want to come back because she was afraid you are gone. She heard at work, so she didn't come back. What should we do? I says, leave her-- I says, if you-- He says to me, I'm not going for her. I said, I'm not expecting you. Don't go. Because she was scared that you are gone. I says, I'm all right. So we dared get out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that aktion, we had a reprieve for a few months, until September '43, the 3rd of September on '43, when both ghettos. At that-- after that aktion, they formed ghetto B, where all the children had to go there, or if parents didn't want to leave the children, they could go with them, and all the old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in Tarnow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Tarnow. It was still in Tarnow. All right, by September 3rd of '43, everybody had to leave, [GERMAN] three of Jews. Three of Jews, actually. So my sister and I went, with our little bundles, whatever was there, a change of clothes, a change of underwear, whatever. And we got to the place. We did not know if we supposed to stand according to work, working place, like we always did in the mornings or in the evenings, whatever it was, was one day you stood. You stood with your group of people that you have to go through, you stood with-- she was being-- she did not work with me. She had to be somewhere else. But we had a funny feeling at that time, let's stay together. Let's stay together. And we held hands. And a Nazi passes us by and just rips the hands apart. She went on one side. I went on the other side with the-- I stood with the Madritch people, and she was on the other side. And before you turned around, these people were being marched. Now that group that remained, whoever worked for Madritch, step forward. And we did. There was a remnant left. And they needed 1,800 people. Because this is what Mr. Madritch wanted. And they counted us. They were short of some people, so they took the rest of the people with us. We stayed overnight on that place, hungry, tired, exhausted, miserable. But fortunately, it was a moonlit night with the star above. And around 2 o'clock, I said to a person that was next to me, you know, I have a very funny feeling deep down in my heart. I said, I feel I'm all alone. My sister was gassed at that time. And next morning, they put us into closed trains, and we wound up in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Closed trains, you mean cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle trains, yes, cattle trains. We went on the-- of course, hundred to a-- to a cattle car. That trip took a whole day. Till this day, I cannot understand that upon-- till this day, I just don't know how it all-- you know, we got there, and the Jewish police, and I recognized a few of them from Krakow, and they just like turned their heads away.

speaker: interviewee
text: One who was married, who I went to school with her cousin, and she knew me, she turned her head away. I says, what kind of people are these? What's going on here? What kind of a place? And the language that they used at us, the language! My god, I've never known-- I've never known these words. And I was not a prude. I'm not a prude, but to hear it so eloquently spoken, and so much, every minute of the day, each one of us, was like treated like an animal. OK, we got there. They searched our whatever we had. This was-- Plaszow was still-- you were still allowed to have your own clothes, were still allowed to keep whatever you had with you. And we had the [NON-ENGLISH], the bath, whatever. And they led us to a barrack. OK. I was given a place. I chose the bottom one, because I felt maybe it's easier this way. Who the heck knows? I don't know. The [INAUDIBLE] was a Jewish girl from Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every morning, we had to go to work. But not to Madritch, because Madritch was not set up yet, the machines. You see, the cattle came, but the machines had to be handled with gloves. So that transport did not arrive. So we had like two weeks in-between where we developed the skills of living in a camp with Jewish couples. Because very few Germans were in that-- inside the camp. It was just the Jewish police that did the job. We had to carry boards from one place to another. And after that place got big with the boards, you had to take those boards to the other place back. We were kept busy 12 hours a day being beaten. I was beaten more by the Jews than I was beaten by Germans. And this went on for about two weeks, three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever wonder why the Jews behaved that way towards other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not even think about it. Why? There was no answer to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no-- I had no reasonable answer. So I didn't even ask that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about it after the war, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I guess they wanted to survive on my back. I wanted to survive too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever confront any after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because many of them did not survive. Many did not survive. Most of them didn't. That's the pity of it. The head of the police and his wife in Plaszow, they were killed by the Nazi-- by Mr. Goeth, who was the-- who was the head of the camp. Not in my time, I was already in Auschwitz. But just before the second transport came to Auschwitz, they had a bad end. And many of their policeman went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue where you left off.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Then Mr. Madritch opened up his factory, and we settled in a life, a routine life, more or less. Winter in Poland is very hard. Between snow, and rain, and drizzle, and not having proper clothes, and not having proper shoes, it was not an easy winter at all. We had no water. From my barrack to the latrine, I could not go. Because you see, I could have been shot. But if I opened up the door in the back and took a little bit of snow, I was able to get washed before the flood light went my way. So we used to keep vigil about the flood light and sneak out, and sneak, and get washed quickly whichever way we could, and get dressed for work. And when I had the night shift, there was not such a problem, because it was mingling with people. So you were able to go get yourself washed. But if I had the morning shift, it was a little harder. And then they brought Hungarian Jews to Plaszow from Auschwitz. And I had an idea what Auschwitz was all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because shaved women-- you see, we couldn't-- when we came from work, and we spotted these people in the upper plats, because we had to be counted, and we saw-- and we said to ourself, who are these people? Are those women? Or are those men in women's clothes? Or are those women, shaved? Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we discovered that we have to share our bunks with those women, that when we were sleeping, they were working. And when we were working, they were sleeping on our bunks. Because there was no more room in there. But you see, this was their way of eliminating people as much as possible, of making life incredible impossible, and bringing disease among us all. Because, as a matter of fact, that was springtime, about April, that they brought a transport from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That they brought them. And like I said, we had usual a Sunday, a few hours off. So the girl that slept on my bed where I slept, she and I began to strike a little bit of a friendship, whatever it is, whatever you call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me, she asked me where my parents are. I says, I don't know. And she says to me, they're not living. And I says, you sure? She says, I know what's going on with the ovens in Auschwitz. But they weren't taken to Auschwitz. She says, so somewheres else were ovens, and I'll prove it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she put up a seance with a Ouija board. You know, the piece of board. I've never witnessed anything like it, and I never believed in that. But the few question that she had answered, that she asked, she got the answer somehow. I don't know how, whether she did it, or whatever. I don't believe in that. But this is when I walked away with the knowledge that I will never see my sisters or my parents again. Because you see, as long as I felt I am alive, I, me and my sisters were young, maybe at least somebody survives under the same condition I'm trying to survive. And I guess that little bit of hope, and a little bit of thought, that maybe, maybe, somebody will survive, pulled me through the toughest time, with the nightmares that I had, or pulled me, maybe, through the tough days, the tough nights. That little bit of that ray of hope, maybe somebody survives. And then in May of '44, we came from work, from the night shift, and we were led into the upper plats. Because Dr. Mengele and his crew were there for a selection. Plaszow did not undergo-- you know, Plaszow had killings on daily basis by Mr. Goeth and Mr. [INAUDIBLE] Young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goeth and Mr. who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young. That was his aide, his helper. And also the other Nazis, they felt like it was some sport. They were pulling people and shoots them, you know. After all, Plaszow was built on the summit on the Jewish cemetery. So what difference does it make?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a minute now to change the tape.
